html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Fourteen — Part One

The Book of Melakhim Aleph

MELAKHIM ALEPH

The First Book of Kings

Chapters 1 through 6

Shelomoh's Succession — Dawid's Last Words — Wisdom at Giv'on — The Temple Built

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

No complete DSS manuscript for Melakhim Aleph — primary witness: Masoretic Text

Septuagint — significant variants in Chapters 1-11

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — Melakhim preserved in full

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 6 — THE KINGDOM OF SHELOMOH ESTABLISHED

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 1 Adoniayah Claims the Throne — Natan and Bathsheva — Shelomoh Anointed King

1 Now King Dawid was old and advanced in years. And although they covered him with clothes, he could not get warm.

2 Therefore his servants said to him, Let a young woman be sought for my lord the king, and let her wait on the king and be in his service. Let her lie in your arms, that my lord the king may be warm.

3 So they sought for a beautiful young woman throughout all the territory of Yisra'EL, and found Avishag the Shunammite, and brought her to the king.

4 The young woman was very beautiful, and she was of service to the king and attended to him, but the king knew her not.

5 Now Adoniyahu the son of Khaggit exalted himself, saying, I will be king. And he prepared for himself chariots and horsemen, and fifty men to run before him.

6 His father had never at any time displeased him by asking, Why have you done thus? He was also a very handsome man, and he was born next after Avshalom.

7 He conferred with Yoav the son of Tzeruya and with Evyatar the priest, and they followed Adoniyahu and helped him.

8 But Tzadok the priest and Benaya the son of Yehoyada and Natan the prophet and Shim'i and Re'i and Dawid's mighty men were not with Adoniyahu.

9 Adoniyahu sacrificed sheep, oxen, and fattened cattle by the Serpent's Stone, which is beside En-Rogel, and he invited all his brothers, the king's sons, and all the royal officials of Yehudah,

10 But he did not invite Natan the prophet or Benaya or the mighty men or Shelomoh his brother.

11 Then Natan said to Bathsheva the mother of Shelomoh, Have you not heard that Adoniyahu the son of Khaggit has become king and Dawid our lord does not know it?

12 Now therefore come, let me give you advice, that you may save your own life and the life of your son Shelomoh.

13 Go in at once to King Dawid, and say to him, Did you not, my lord the king, swear to your servant, saying, Shelomoh your son shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne? Why then is Adoniyahu king?

14 Then while you are still speaking with the king, I also will come in after you and confirm your words.

15 So Bathsheva went to the king in his chamber. Now the king was very old, and Avishag the Shunammite was attending to the king.

16 Bathsheva bowed and paid homage to the king, and the king said, What do you desire?

17 She said to him, My lord, you swore to your servant by YHWH your Creator, saying, Shelomoh your son shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne.

18 And now, behold, Adoniyahu is king, although you, my lord the king, do not know it.

19 He has sacrificed oxen, fattened cattle, and sheep in abundance, and has invited all the sons of the king, Evyatar the priest, and Yoav the commander of the army, but Shelomoh your servant he has not invited.

20 And now, my lord the king, the eyes of all Yisra'EL are on you, to tell them who shall sit on the throne of my lord the king after him.

21 Otherwise it will come to pass, when my lord the king sleeps with his fathers, that I and my son Shelomoh will be counted offenders.

22 While she was still speaking with the king, Natan the prophet came in.

23 And they told the king, Here is Natan the prophet. And when he came in before the king, he bowed before the king with his face to the ground.

24 And Natan said, My lord the king, have you said, Adoniyahu shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne?

25 For he has gone down this day and has sacrificed oxen, fattened cattle, and sheep in abundance and has invited all the king's sons, the commanders of the army, and Evyatar the priest. And behold, they are eating and drinking before him, and saying, Long live King Adoniyahu.

26 But me, even me your servant, and Tzadok the priest, and Benaya the son of Yehoyada, and your servant Shelomoh he has not invited.

27 Has this thing been brought about by my lord the king and you have not told your servants who should sit on the throne of my lord the king after him?

28 Then King Dawid answered, Call Bathsheva to me. So she came into the king's presence and stood before the king.

29 And the king swore, saying, As YHWH lives, who has redeemed my soul out of every adversity,

30 As I swore to you by YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, saying, Shelomoh your son shall reign after me, and he shall sit on my throne in my place, even so will I do this day.

31 Then Bathsheva bowed with her face to the ground and paid homage to the king and said, May my lord King Dawid live forever.

32 King Dawid said, Call to me Tzadok the priest, Natan the prophet, and Benaya the son of Yehoyada. So they came before the king.

33 And the king said to them, Take with you the servants of your lord and have Shelomoh my son ride on my own mule, and bring him down to Gikhon.

34 And let Tzadok the priest and Natan the prophet there anoint him king over Yisra'EL. Then blow the trumpet and say, Long live King Shelomoh.

35 You shall then come up after him, and he shall come and sit on my throne, for he shall be king in my place. And I have appointed him to be ruler over Yisra'EL and over Yehudah.

36 And Benaya the son of Yehoyada answered the king and said, Amen. May YHWH, the Creator of my lord the king, say so.

37 As YHWH has been with my lord the king, even so may he be with Shelomoh, and make his throne greater than the throne of my lord King Dawid.

38 So Tzadok the priest, Natan the prophet, and Benaya the son of Yehoyada, and the Kerethites and the Pelethites went down and had Shelomoh ride on King Dawid's mule and brought him to Gikhon.

39 There Tzadok the priest took the horn of oil from the tent and anointed Shelomoh. Then they blew the trumpet, and all the people said, Long live King Shelomoh.

40 And all the people went up after him, playing on pipes, and rejoicing with great joy, so that the earth was split by their noise.

41 Adoniyahu and all the guests who were with him heard it as they finished feasting. And when Yoav heard the sound of the trumpet, he said, What does this uproar in the city mean?

42 While he was still speaking, behold, Yonatan the son of Evyatar the priest came. And Adoniyahu said, Come in, for you are a worthy man and bring good news.

43 Yonatan answered Adoniyahu, No, for our lord King Dawid has made Shelomoh king,

44 And the king has sent with him Tzadok the priest, Natan the prophet, and Benaya the son of Yehoyada, and the Kerethites and the Pelethites. And they had him ride on the king's mule.

45 And Tzadok the priest and Natan the prophet have anointed him king at Gikhon, and they have gone up from there rejoicing, so that the city is in an uproar. This is the noise that you have heard.

46 Shelomoh sits on the royal throne.

47 Moreover, the king's servants came to congratulate our lord King Dawid, saying, Your Creator make the name of Shelomoh more famous than yours, and make his throne greater than your throne. And the king bowed himself on the bed.

48 And the king also said, Blessed be YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, who has granted someone to sit on my throne this day, my own eyes seeing it.

49 Then all the guests of Adoniyahu trembled and rose, and each went his own way.

50 And Adoniyahu feared Shelomoh. So he arose and went and took hold of the horns of the altar.

51 Then it was told Shelomoh, Behold, Adoniyahu fears King Shelomoh, for behold, he has laid hold of the horns of the altar, saying, Let King Shelomoh swear to me first that he will not put his servant to death with the sword.

52 And Shelomoh said, If he will show himself a worthy man, not one of his hairs shall fall to the earth, but if wickedness is found in him, he shall die.

53 So King Shelomoh sent, and they brought him down from the altar. And he came and paid homage to King Shelomoh, and Shelomoh said to him, Go to your house.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. The primary textual witnesses are the Masoretic Text, the Septuagint (which contains significant variants particularly in Chapters 1-11), and the Old Latin tradition. The Orit Ge'ez preserves the full text of Melakhim Aleph as part of the canonical Nevi'im. Chapter 1 completes the succession narrative begun in Shemu'el Bet — the Davidic covenant of Chapter 7 is fulfilled through the anointing of Shelomoh.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Melakhim Aleph in full. The anointing of Shelomoh at Gikhon — verse 39 — parallels Dawid's anointing at Khevron. The covenant continuity from Dawid to Shelomoh is established through the same covenant instruments: the horn of oil, the trumpet, and the covenant witnesses.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness for Melakhim Aleph. The Septuagint contains a significantly different arrangement of material in Chapters 1-11, including an additional account of Yarovam's rise. This compilation follows the Masoretic text order as the received tradition, noting significant Septuagint variants where relevant.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 29-30 — as YHWH lives who has redeemed my soul out of every adversity — as I swore to you by YHWH — Shelomoh your son shall reign after me — even so will I do this day. Dawid's deathbed oath to Bathsheva is the covenant act that sets the succession. His entire reign — from the shepherd boy to the dying king — is framed by YHWH's redemption from every adversity. Verse 50 — Adoniyahu feared Shelomoh and took hold of the horns of the altar. The sanctuary as the place of covenant refuge even for those who attempted to seize the throne. Shelomoh's initial covenant mercy — verse 52-53 — conditional on Adoniyahu's conduct — is the beginning of a covenant test Adoniyahu will fail in Chapter 2.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 2 Dawid's Final Charge to Shelomoh — Dawid Dies — Adoniyahu Executed — Yoav Executed — Shim'i Executed

1 When Dawid's time to die drew near, he commanded Shelomoh his son, saying,

2 I am about to go the way of all the earth. Be strong, and show yourself a man,

3 And keep the charge of YHWH your Creator, walking in his ways and keeping his statutes, his commandments, his rules, and his testimonies, as it is written in the Torah of Moshe, that you may prosper in all that you do and wherever you turn,

4 That YHWH may establish his word that he spoke concerning me, saying, If your sons pay close attention to their way, to walk before me in faithfulness with all their heart and with all their soul, you shall not lack a man on the throne of Yisra'EL.

5 Moreover, you also know what Yoav the son of Tzeruya did to me, what he did to the two commanders of the armies of Yisra'EL, to Avner the son of Ner and to Amasa the son of Yiter, whom he killed, avenging in time of peace for blood that had been shed in war, and putting the blood of war on the belt around his waist and on the sandals on his feet.

6 Act therefore according to your wisdom, but do not let his gray head go down to Sheol in peace.

7 But deal loyally with the sons of Barzillai the Gil'adite, and let them be among those who eat at your table, for with such loyalty they met me when I fled from Avshalom your brother.

8 And there is also with you Shim'i the son of Gera, the Binyaminite from Bakhurim, who cursed me with a grievous curse on the day when I went to Makhanayim. But when he came down to meet me at the Yarden, I swore to him by YHWH, saying, I will not put you to death with the sword.

9 Now therefore do not hold him guiltless, for you are a wise man. You will know what you ought to do to him, and you shall bring his gray head down with blood to Sheol.

10 Then Dawid slept with his fathers and was buried in the city of Dawid.

11 And the time that Dawid reigned over Yisra'EL was forty years. He reigned seven years in Khevron and thirty-three years in Yerushalayim.

12 So Shelomoh sat on the throne of Dawid his father, and his kingdom was firmly established.

13 Then Adoniyahu the son of Khaggit came to Bathsheva the mother of Shelomoh. And she said, Do you come peaceably? He said, Peaceably.

14 Then he said, I have something to say to you. She said, Speak.

15 He said, You know that the kingdom was mine, and that all Yisra'EL fully expected me to reign. However, the kingdom has turned about and become my brother's, for it was his from YHWH.

16 And now I have one request to make of you — do not refuse me. She said to him, Speak.

17 And he said, Please ask King Shelomoh — he will not refuse you — to give me Avishag the Shunammite as my wife.

18 Bathsheva said, Very well — I will speak for you to the king.

19 So Bathsheva went to King Shelomoh to speak to him on behalf of Adoniyahu. And the king rose to meet her and bowed down to her. Then he sat on his throne and had a seat brought for the king's mother, and she sat on his right.

20 Then she said, I have one small request to make of you — do not refuse me. And the king said to her, Make your request, my mother, for I will not refuse you.

21 She said, Let Avishag the Shunammite be given to Adoniyahu your brother as his wife.

22 King Shelomoh answered his mother, And why do you ask Avishag the Shunammite for Adoniyahu? Ask for him the kingdom also, for he is my older brother, and on his side are Evyatar the priest and Yoav the son of Tzeruya.

23 Then King Shelomoh swore by YHWH, saying, Creator do so to me and more also if this word does not cost Adoniyahu his life.

24 Now therefore as YHWH lives, who has established me and placed me on the throne of Dawid my father, and who has made me a house, as he promised, Adoniyahu shall be put to death this day.

25 So King Shelomoh sent Benaya the son of Yehoyada, and he struck him down, and he died.

26 And to Evyatar the priest the king said, Go to Anathoth, to your own fields, for you deserve death. But I will not at this time put you to death, because you carried the Ark of the Lord YHWH before Dawid my father, and because you shared in all my father's affliction.

27 So Shelomoh expelled Evyatar from being priest to YHWH, thus fulfilling the word of YHWH that he had spoken concerning the house of Eli at Shiloh.

28 When the news came to Yoav — for Yoav had supported Adoniyahu although he had not supported Avshalom — Yoav fled to the tent of YHWH and caught hold of the horns of the altar.

29 And it was told King Shelomoh, Yoav has fled to the tent of YHWH, and behold, he is beside the altar. Then Shelomoh sent Benaya the son of Yehoyada, saying, Go, strike him down.

30 So Benaya came to the tent of YHWH and said to him, The king commands, Come out. But he said, No, I will die here. Then Benaya brought the king word again, saying, Thus said Yoav, and thus he answered me.

31 The king replied to him, Do as he has said, strike him down and bury him, and thus take away from me and from my father's house the guilt for the blood that Yoav shed without cause.

32 YHWH will bring back his bloody deeds on his own head, because, without the knowledge of my father Dawid, he attacked and killed with the sword two men more righteous and better than himself, Avner the son of Ner, commander of the army of Yisra'EL, and Amasa the son of Yiter, commander of the army of Yehudah.

33 So shall their blood come back on the head of Yoav and on the head of his descendants forever. But for Dawid and for his descendants and for his house and for his throne, there shall be peace from YHWH forevermore.

34 Then Benaya the son of Yehoyada went up and struck him down and put him to death. And he was buried in his own house in the wilderness.

35 The king put Benaya the son of Yehoyada over the army in place of Yoav, and the king put Tzadok the priest in the place of Evyatar.

36 Then the king sent and summoned Shim'i and said to him, Build yourself a house in Yerushalayim and dwell there, and do not go from there to any place whatever.

37 For on the day you go out and cross the brook Kidron, know for certain that you shall die. Your blood shall be on your own head.

38 And Shim'i said to the king, What you say is good — as my lord the king has said, so will your servant do. So Shim'i lived in Yerushalayim many days.

39 But it happened at the end of three years that two of Shim'i's servants ran away to Akhish the son of Ma'akhah, king of Gat. And when it was told Shim'i, Behold, your servants are at Gat,

40 Shim'i arose and saddled a donkey and went to Gat to Akhish to seek his servants. Shim'i went and brought his servants from Gat.

41 And when Shelomoh was told that Shim'i had gone from Yerushalayim to Gat and returned,

42 The king sent and summoned Shim'i and said to him, Did I not make you swear by YHWH and solemnly warn you, saying, Know for certain that on the day you go out and go to any place whatever, you shall die? And you said to me, What you say is good — I will obey.

43 Why then have you not kept your oath to YHWH and the commandment with which I commanded you?

44 The king also said to Shim'i, You know in your own heart all the harm that you did to Dawid my father. So YHWH will bring back your harm on your own head.

45 But King Shelomoh shall be blessed, and the throne of Dawid shall be established before YHWH forever.

46 Then the king commanded Benaya the son of Yehoyada, and he went out and struck him down, and he died. So the kingdom was established in the hand of Shelomoh.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Dawid's final charge to Shelomoh — verses 2-9 — is the covenant testament of the dying king. The charge has two parts: the covenant obligation — keep the Torah of Moshe — and the covenant business — settle the accounts with Yoav, honor Barzillai's sons, and deal with Shim'i.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 3 — keep the charge of YHWH your Creator, walking in his ways and keeping his statutes, his commandments, his rules, and his testimonies, as it is written in the Torah of Moshe — is read as the covenant summary of the entire Devarim theology applied to the monarchy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 27 — Shelomoh expelled Evyatar, fulfilling the word of YHWH spoken concerning the house of Eli at Shiloh — is the covenant long memory of the Nevi'im: the prophecy against Eli's house in Shemu'el Aleph 2:27-36 is fulfilled here, generations later, in the establishment of Shelomoh's kingdom.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2-4 — be strong, show yourself a man, keep the charge of YHWH, walk in his ways, keep his Torah — that YHWH may establish his word concerning me — if your sons pay close attention to walk before me in faithfulness, you shall not lack a man on the throne of Yisra'EL. Dawid's final words encode the covenant condition of the Davidic dynasty: the eternal covenant of Chapter 7 is unconditional in its ultimate scope but conditional in its immediate expressions. The throne endures but individual kings must walk in the Torah. Verse 46 — so the kingdom was established in the hand of Shelomoh. The covenant closure of the succession narrative.

SHELOMOH'S WISDOM AND THE TEMPLE — CHAPTERS 3 THROUGH 6

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 3 Shelomoh's Prayer for Wisdom — Give Your Servant an Understanding Heart — Two Women and One Child

1 Shelomoh made a marriage alliance with Pharaoh king of Egypt. He took Pharaoh's daughter and brought her into the city of Dawid until he had finished building his own house and the house of YHWH and the wall around Yerushalayim.

2 The people were sacrificing at the high places, however, because no house had yet been built for the name of YHWH.

3 Shelomoh loved YHWH, walking in the statutes of Dawid his father, only he sacrificed and made offerings at the high places.

4 And the king went to Giv'on to sacrifice there, for that was the great high place. Shelomoh used to offer a thousand burnt offerings on that altar.

5 At Giv'on YHWH appeared to Shelomoh in a dream by night, and Creator said, Ask what I shall give you.

6 And Shelomoh said, You have shown great and steadfast love to your servant Dawid my father, because he walked before you in faithfulness, in righteousness, and in uprightness of heart toward you. And you have kept for him this great and steadfast love and have given him a son to sit on his throne this day.

7 And now, O YHWH my Creator, you have made your servant king in place of Dawid my father, although I am but a little child. I do not know how to go out or come in.

8 And your servant is in the midst of your people whom you have chosen, a great people, too many to be numbered or counted for multitude.

9 Give your servant therefore an understanding heart to govern your people, that I may discern between good and evil, for who is able to govern this your great people?

10 It pleased the Lord that Shelomoh had asked this.

11 And Creator said to him, Because you have asked this, and have not asked for yourself long life or riches or the life of your enemies, but have asked for yourself understanding to discern what is right,

12 Behold, I now do according to your word. Behold, I give you a wise and discerning heart, so that none like you has been before you and none like you shall arise after you.

13 I give you also what you have not asked, both riches and honor, so that no other king shall compare with you, all your days.

14 And if you will walk in my ways, keeping my statutes and my commandments, as your father Dawid walked, then I will lengthen your days.

15 And Shelomoh awoke, and behold, it was a dream. Then he came to Yerushalayim and stood before the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH, and offered up burnt offerings and peace offerings, and made a feast for all his servants.

16 Then two women who were prostitutes came to the king and stood before him.

17 The one woman said, Oh, my lord, this woman and I live in the same house, and I gave birth to a child while she was in the house.

18 Then on the third day after I gave birth, this woman also gave birth. And we were alone. There was no one else with us in the house — only we two were in the house.

19 And this woman's son died in the night, because she lay on him.

20 And she arose at midnight and took my son from beside me, while your servant slept, and laid him at her breast, and laid her dead son at my breast.

21 When I rose in the morning to nurse my child, behold, he was dead. But when I looked at him closely in the morning, behold, he was not the child that I had borne.

22 But the other woman said, No, the living child is mine, and the dead child is yours. The first said, No, the dead child is yours, and the living child is mine. Thus they spoke before the king.

23 Then the king said, The one says, This is my son that is alive, and your son is dead, and the other says, No, but your son is dead, and my son is the living one.

24 And the king said, Bring me a sword. So a sword was brought before the king.

25 And the king said, Divide the living child in two, and give half to the one and half to the other.

26 Then the woman whose son was alive said to the king, because her heart yearned for her son, Oh, my lord, give her the living child, and by no means put him to death. But the other said, He shall be neither mine nor yours — divide him.

27 Then the king answered and said, Give the living child to the first woman, and by no means put him to death — she is his mother.

28 And all Yisra'EL heard of the judgment that the king had rendered, and they stood in awe of the king, because they perceived that the wisdom of Creator was in him to do justice.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. The Giv'on dream — verses 5-15 — is the covenant calling of Shelomoh's wisdom. YHWH appears in a dream and offers anything. Shelomoh's request for an understanding heart — lev shome'a — a hearing heart — is the covenant disposition of the ideal king: not power or wealth or vengeance but the capacity to discern between good and evil in governing YHWH's people.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. Shelomoh's response to YHWH's offer — verse 7-9 — I am but a little child — I do not know how to go out or come in — give your servant an understanding heart — is read as the covenant model of wise leadership: the acknowledgment of personal insufficiency as the ground for asking YHWH's wisdom.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 14 — if you will walk in my ways, keeping my statutes and my commandments as your father Dawid walked, then I will lengthen your days — the covenant condition within the gift of wisdom. The wisdom is given unconditionally. The length of days is conditional on covenant faithfulness. The distinction matters for the Shelomoh narrative.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — give your servant an understanding heart to govern your people, that I may discern between good and evil. The wisdom Shelomoh requests is not abstract intelligence but covenant judicial capacity: the ability to discern good from evil in the service of YHWH's people. Verse 11-13 — because you asked for wisdom rather than wealth, long life, or vengeance — I give you wisdom greater than any before or after — and also the things you did not ask. The covenant principle: seek YHWH's kingdom and these things are added — Mattityahu 6:33 echoes this covenant logic. The king who asked for nothing but the capacity to serve receives everything he did not ask for.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 4 Shelomoh's Officials — His Provisions — His Wisdom Exceeded All — He Spoke of Trees and Beasts

1 King Shelomoh was king over all Yisra'EL,

2 And these were his officials: Azaryah the son of Tzadok was the priest.

3 Elikhоref and Akhiyah the sons of Shisha were secretaries. Yehoshafat the son of Akhilud was the recorder.

4 Benaya the son of Yehoyada was in command of the army. Tzadok and Evyatar were priests.

5 Azaryah the son of Natan was over the officers. Zavud the son of Natan was priest and king's friend.

6 Akhishar was in charge of the palace. And Adoniram the son of Avda was in charge of the forced labor.

7 Shelomoh had twelve officers over all Yisra'EL, who provided food for the king and his household. Each man had to make provision for one month in the year.

8 These were their names: Ben-Khur, in the hill country of Efrayim.

9 Ben-Dekar, in Maqatz, Sha'alvim, Beit-Shemesh, and Elon-Beit-Khanan.

10 Ben-Khesed, in Arubot — to him belonged Sokhoh and all the land of Khefer.

11 Ben-Avina'dav, in all Naphot-Dor. He had Tafat, Shelomoh's daughter, as his wife.

12 Ba'anah the son of Akhilud, in Ta'anakh, Megiddo, and all Beit-She'an that is beside Tzarethan below Yizre'el, and from Beit-She'an to Avel-Mekholah, as far as the other side of Yokne'am.

13 Ben-Gever, in Ramot-Gil'ad — to him belonged the towns of Ya'ir the son of Menasheh, which are in Gil'ad, and he had the region of Argov, which is in Bashan, sixty great cities with walls and bronze bars.

14 Akhina'dav the son of Iddo, in Makhanayim.

15 Akhima'atz, in Naftali — he also had taken Basmat, Shelomoh's daughter, as his wife.

16 Ba'anah the son of Khushai, in Asher and Bealot.

17 Yehoshafat the son of Paruakh, in Yissaskhar.

18 Shim'i the son of Ela, in Binyamin.

19 Gever the son of Uri, in the land of Gil'ad, the country of Sikhon king of the Amorites and of Og king of Bashan. And there was one governor who was over the land.

20 Yehudah and Yisra'EL were as many as the sand by the sea. They ate and drank and were happy.

21 Shelomoh ruled over all the kingdoms from the Euphrates to the land of the Pelishtim and to the border of Egypt. They brought tribute and served Shelomoh all the days of his life.

22 Shelomoh's provision for one day was thirty cors of fine flour and sixty cors of meal,

23 Ten fat oxen, and twenty pasture-fed cattle, a hundred sheep, besides deer, gazelles, roebucks, and fattened fowl.

24 For he had dominion over all the region west of the Euphrates from Tifsakha to Azzah, over all the kings west of the Euphrates. And he had peace on all sides around him.

25 And Yehudah and Yisra'EL lived in safety, from Dan even to Be'er-Sheva, every man under his vine and under his fig tree, all the days of Shelomoh.

26 Shelomoh also had forty thousand stalls of horses for his chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen.

27 And those officers supplied provisions for King Shelomoh, and for all who came to King Shelomoh's table, each one in his month. They let nothing be lacking.

28 Barley also and straw for the horses and swift steeds they brought to the place where it was required, each according to his duty.

29 And Creator gave Shelomoh wisdom and understanding beyond measure, and breadth of mind like the sand on the seashore,

30 So that Shelomoh's wisdom surpassed the wisdom of all the people of the east and all the wisdom of Egypt.

31 For he was wiser than all other men, wiser than Ethan the Ezrakhite, and Heman, Kalkol, and Darda, the sons of Makhol, and his fame was in all the surrounding nations.

32 He also spoke three thousand proverbs, and his songs were a thousand and five.

33 He spoke of trees, from the cedar that is in Lebanon to the hyssop that grows out of the wall. He spoke also of beasts, and of birds, and of reptiles, and of fish.

34 And people of all nations came to hear the wisdom of Shelomoh, and from all the kings of the earth, who had heard of his wisdom.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. The administrative structure of Shelomoh's kingdom — twelve officers over all Yisra'EL, each providing provisions for one month — reflects the covenant organization of the united monarchy at its peak. The covenant promise to Avraham — as many as the sand by the sea — verse 20 — is described as fulfilled in Shelomoh's reign.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Verse 25 — Yehudah and Yisra'EL lived in safety, every man under his vine and under his fig tree, all the days of Shelomoh — is read as the covenant fulfillment of the Mosaic blessing of Devarim 8: the peace and provision of covenant obedience expressed in the image of the settled farmer.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verses 29-34 — Creator gave Shelomoh wisdom beyond measure — he spoke three thousand proverbs and a thousand and five songs — of trees, beasts, birds, reptiles, and fish — all nations came to hear his wisdom. The wisdom catalog positions Shelomoh as the covenant universal sage whose knowledge encompasses the entire created order.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 29 — Creator gave Shelomoh wisdom and understanding beyond measure, and breadth of mind like the sand on the seashore. The same covenant image used for the multitude of Yisra'EL in verse 20 — like the sand by the sea — is now applied to the breadth of Shelomoh's mind. The covenant king's wisdom is as vast as the covenant people he governs. Verse 33 — he spoke of trees from the cedar of Lebanon to the hyssop in the wall. The covenant wisdom of Shelomoh encompasses the full range of creation — from the greatest to the smallest. This is the wisdom theology that connects Mishlei, Kohelet, and Iyov: the observation of creation as the disclosure of the Creator's character.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 5 Khiram of Tzor and the Temple Agreement — Shelomoh's Labor Force — Cedar and Cypress for the House

1 Now Khiram king of Tzor sent his servants to Shelomoh when he heard that they had anointed him king in place of his father, for Khiram always loved Dawid.

2 And Shelomoh sent word to Khiram,

3 You know that Dawid my father could not build a house for the name of YHWH his Creator because of the warfare with which his enemies surrounded him, until YHWH put them under the soles of his feet.

4 But now YHWH my Creator has given me rest on every side. There is neither adversary nor misfortune.

5 And so I intend to build a house for the name of YHWH my Creator, as YHWH said to Dawid my father, Your son, whom I will set on your throne in your place, shall build the house for my name.

6 Now therefore command that cedars of Lebanon be cut for me. And my servants will join your servants, and I will pay you for your servants whatever wages you set, for you know that there is no one among us who knows how to cut timber like the Tzidonians.

7 As soon as Khiram heard the words of Shelomoh, he rejoiced greatly and said, Blessed be YHWH this day, who has given to Dawid a wise son to be over this great people.

8 And Khiram sent to Shelomoh, saying, I have heard the message that you have sent to me. I am ready to do all you desire in the matter of cedar and cypress timber.

9 My servants shall bring it down to the sea from Lebanon, and I will make it into rafts to go by sea to the place you direct. And I will have them broken up there, and you shall receive it. And you shall meet my wishes by providing food for my household.

10 So Khiram supplied Shelomoh with all the timber of cedar and cypress that he desired,

11 While Shelomoh gave Khiram twenty thousand cors of wheat as food for his household, and twenty thousand cors of beaten oil. Shelomoh gave this to Khiram year by year.

12 And YHWH gave Shelomoh wisdom, as he promised him. And there was peace between Khiram and Shelomoh, and the two of them made a treaty.

13 King Shelomoh drafted forced labor out of all Yisra'EL, and the draft numbered thirty thousand men.

14 And he sent them to Lebanon, ten thousand a month in shifts. They would be a month in Lebanon and two months at home. Adoniram was in charge of the draft.

15 Shelomoh also had seventy thousand burden-bearers and eighty thousand quarrymen in the hill country,

16 Besides Shelomoh's three thousand three hundred chief officers who were over the work, who had charge of the people who carried on the work.

17 At the king's command they quarried out great, costly stones in order to lay the foundation of the house with dressed stones.

18 So Shelomoh's builders and Khiram's builders and the men of Gebal did the cutting and prepared the timber and the stone to build the house.

19 In the four hundred and eightieth year after the people of Yisra'EL came out of the land of Egypt, in the fourth year of Shelomoh's reign over Yisra'EL, in the month of Ziv, which is the second month, he began to build the house of YHWH.

20 The house that King Shelomoh built for YHWH was sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high.

21 The vestibule in front of the nave of the house was twenty cubits long, equal to the width of the house, and ten cubits deep in front of the house.

22 And he made for the house windows with recessed frames.

23 He also built a structure against the wall of the house, running around the walls of the house, both the nave and the inner sanctuary. And he made side chambers all around.

24 The lowest story was five cubits broad, the middle one was six cubits broad, and the third was seven cubits broad. For around the outside of the house he made offsets on the wall in order that the supporting beams should not be inserted into the walls of the house.

25 When the house was built, it was with stone prepared at the quarry, so that neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron was heard in the house while it was being built.

26 The entrance for the lowest story was on the south side of the house, and one went up by stairs to the middle story, and from the middle story to the third.

27 So he built the house and finished it, and he made the ceiling of the house of beams and planks of cedar.

28 He built the structure against the whole house, five cubits high, and it was joined to the house with timbers of cedar.

29 The word of YHWH came to Shelomoh,

30 Concerning this house that you are building, if you will walk in my statutes and obey my rules and keep all my commandments and walk in them, then I will establish my word with you, which I spoke to Dawid your father.

31 And I will dwell among the children of Yisra'EL and will not forsake my people Yisra'EL.

32 So Shelomoh built the house and finished it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. The temple construction narrative is one of the most detailed building accounts in the Nevi'im. Verse 19 — in the four hundred and eightieth year after the people of Yisra'EL came out of Egypt — is the primary chronological anchor for the entire period from the Exodus to the temple construction.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. The covenant significance of the temple project — verse 3-5 — is that Dawid could not build because of the warfare surrounding him but Shelomoh builds in the rest that YHWH promised. The house is built in the covenant rest, not in the covenant warfare.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verses 29-31 — the word of YHWH to Shelomoh during the building — if you walk in my statutes and keep my commandments, I will establish my word and dwell among the children of Yisra'EL — is the covenant condition stated again in the midst of the building project. The house is being built, but the condition for YHWH's presence in it is covenant obedience.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25 — the house was built with stone prepared at the quarry, so that no hammer or axe or any tool of iron was heard in the house while it was being built. The covenant building of the sacred space requires that no tool of violence — iron implements of war — be heard within it. This is consistent with the altar law of Shemot 20:25 — do not build an altar of hewn stone, for by wielding a tool on it you profane it. The covenant sanctuary is built in silence and peace. Verse 30-31 — the covenant condition for YHWH's dwelling among Yisra'EL is Shelomoh's personal covenant faithfulness. The house of cedar will not guarantee the presence of YHWH — only the covenant life of the king and people can do that.

MELAKHIM ALEPH — CHAPTER 6 The Building of the Temple — The Inner Sanctuary — The Cherubim — Seven Years to Build

1 In the four hundred and eightieth year after the people of Yisra'EL came out of the land of Egypt, in the fourth year of Shelomoh's reign over Yisra'EL, in the month of Ziv, which is the second month, he began to build the house of YHWH.

2 The house that King Shelomoh built for YHWH was sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high.

3 The vestibule in front of the nave of the house was twenty cubits long, equal to the width of the house, and ten cubits deep in front of the house.

4 And he made for the house windows with recessed frames.

5 He also built a structure against the wall of the house, running around the walls of the house, both the nave and the inner sanctuary. And he made side chambers all around.

6 The lowest story was five cubits broad, the middle one was six cubits broad, and the third was seven cubits broad. For around the outside of the house he made offsets on the wall in order that the supporting beams should not be inserted into the walls of the house.

7 When the house was built, it was with stone prepared at the quarry, so that neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron was heard in the house while it was being built.

8 The entrance for the lowest story was on the south side of the house, and one went up by stairs to the middle story, and from the middle story to the third.

9 So he built the house and finished it, and he made the ceiling of the house of beams and planks of cedar.

10 He built the structure against the whole house, five cubits high, and it was joined to the house with timbers of cedar.

11 Now the word of YHWH came to Shelomoh,

12 Concerning this house that you are building, if you will walk in my statutes and obey my rules and keep all my commandments and walk in them, then I will establish my word with you, which I spoke to Dawid your father.

13 And I will dwell among the children of Yisra'EL and will not forsake my people Yisra'EL.

14 So Shelomoh built the house and finished it.

15 He lined the walls of the house on the inside with boards of cedar. From the floor of the house to the walls of the ceiling, he covered them on the inside with wood, and he covered the floor of the house with boards of cypress.

16 He built twenty cubits of the rear of the house with boards of cedar from the floor to the walls, and he built this within as an inner sanctuary, as the Most Holy Place.

17 The house, that is, the nave in front of the inner sanctuary, was forty cubits long.

18 The cedar within the house was carved in the form of gourds and open flowers. All was cedar — no stone was seen.

19 The inner sanctuary he prepared in the innermost part of the house, to set there the Ark of the Covenant of YHWH.

20 The inner sanctuary was twenty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and twenty cubits high, and he overlaid it with pure gold. He also overlaid the altar of cedar.

21 And Shelomoh overlaid the inside of the house with pure gold, and he drew chains of gold across, in front of the inner sanctuary, and overlaid it with gold.

22 And he overlaid the whole house with gold, until all the house was finished. Also the whole altar that belonged to the inner sanctuary he overlaid with gold.

23 In the inner sanctuary he made two cherubim of olivewood, each ten cubits high.

24 Five cubits was the length of one wing of the cherub, and five cubits the length of the other wing of the cherub — it was ten cubits from the tip of one wing to the tip of the other.

25 The other cherub also measured ten cubits — both cherubim had the same measure and the same form.

26 The height of one cherub was ten cubits, and so was the other cherub.

27 He put the cherubim in the innermost part of the house. And the wings of the cherubim were spread out so that a wing of one touched the one wall, and a wing of the other cherub touched the other wall — their other wings touched each other in the middle of the house.

28 And he overlaid the cherubim with gold.

29 Around all the walls of the house he carved engraved figures of cherubim and palm trees and open flowers, in the inner and outer rooms.

30 The floor of the house he overlaid with gold in the inner and outer rooms.

31 For the entrance to the inner sanctuary he made doors of olivewood — the lintel and the doorposts were five-sided.

32 He covered the two doors of olivewood with carvings of cherubim, palm trees, and open flowers. He overlaid them with gold and spread gold on the cherubim and on the palm trees.

33 So also he made for the entrance to the nave doorposts of olivewood, in the form of a square,

34 And two doors of cypress wood. The two leaves of the one door were folding, and the two leaves of the other door were folding.

35 On them he carved cherubim and palm trees and open flowers, and he overlaid them with gold evenly applied on the carved work.

36 He built the inner court with three courses of dressed stone and one course of cedar beams.

37 In the fourth year the foundation of the house of YHWH was laid, in the month of Ziv.

38 And in the eleventh year, in the month of Bul, which is the eighth month, the house was finished in all its parts, and according to all its specifications. He was seven years in building it.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] No DSS manuscript of Melakhim Aleph survives. Chapter 6 is the most detailed architectural description of the First Temple in the Nevi'im. The inner sanctuary — the Devir, the Most Holy Place — was a perfect cube of twenty cubits — verse 20 — housing the Ark of the Covenant under the wings of the two great cherubim.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. The covenant word that comes to Shelomoh during the construction — verses 11-13 — is read as the interruption of the building account with the covenant condition: the house of cedar will not guarantee YHWH's presence. Only covenant faithfulness can do that.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Text is the primary witness. Verse 38 — seven years in building it. The sacred number of completion for the covenant house. The temple is built in covenant time — seven years — on covenant ground — the threshing floor Dawid purchased at the end of Shemu'el Bet — with covenant materials — cedar from Lebanon — and completed in covenant silence — no iron tool heard.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11-13 — the word of YHWH interrupts the building account at the mid-point. The architectural detail is meticulous and extensive. YHWH interrupts it with a single covenant condition: if you walk in my statutes — I will establish my word — I will dwell among the children of Yisra'EL — I will not forsake my people. The house is impressive. YHWH's presence in it depends on nothing about the house. It depends entirely on the covenant faithfulness of the king and people who worship in it. The building of the temple is not the guarantee of YHWH's blessing — it is the occasion for the reiteration of the covenant condition.

END OF PART ONE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 6
